meeting_context: City of Mountain View Visual Arts Committee meeting covering approval of prior minutes, future agenda planning, Castro Street pedestrian mall updates, CIP public art project updates, continued review of the draft public art strategy, the 2023 Center for the Performing Arts Call for Artists approach, authorization of virtual meetings under AB 361, and adoption of the 2023 meeting schedule. Discussion also focused on supporting local artists, exhibition opportunities, and lessons from Pyramid Park public art siting.

points.text: John Lang reported that Susie Merrar notified staff in advance that she was traveling and would be recorded as an excused absence.
points.source: John Lang: Susie did contact me in advance of the meeting. John Lang: Let me know she is out travelling. John Lang: This will be an excused absence.
points.speaker: John Lang

points.text: The committee approved the September 14 meeting minutes by roll-call vote after a motion by Don Whitebread and a second by Regina Sakols.
points.source: Don Whitebread: Can I make that motion. Regina Skalos: I'll second. Jesse Cupp: Can we get a roll call vote please? Jesse Cupp: All right, motion passes.
points.speaker: Jesse Cupp

points.text: No members of the public were present for public comment, so the committee moved on without public communications.
points.source: John Lang: At the present time we do not have any members of the public joining, so we can move on.
points.speaker: John Lang

topics.topic: Future agenda items and K5 policy refinement

points.text: John Lang said staff would continue the public art strategy discussion in November, bring Bruce England back to discuss art placement and landscaping integration, and keep exploring placemaking opportunities connected to pedestrian and capital improvement programs.
points.source: John Lang: One is we'll bring back more work in the November agenda on public art strategy. John Lang: I do plan to bring back Bruce next meeting to talk about our placement landscaping integrations. John Lang: And then the third item is continuing to explore placemaking opportunities regarding the pedestrian, long interim and long term capital improvement programs.
points.speaker: John Lang

points.text: Jesse Cupp proposed revisiting the K5 policy by working backward from real CIP examples such as bike infrastructure and McKelvey Ballpark so the policy language better matches the committee's intent for which projects should qualify for public art.
points.source: Jesse Cupp: I think we need to go back at some point and update the K5 again just to get up to date with our intentions. Jesse Cupp: I think what we need to do is kind of work backwards from real CIP projects. Jesse Cupp: What language do we need to include to make sure that it's public art because it didn't qualify? Jesse Cupp: Or if we look at Mckelvey Ballpark, that didn't qualify.
points.speaker: Jesse Cupp

points.text: John Lang reported that City Council approved resolutions to permanently close Castro Street, with ordinance adoption scheduled for the October 25 council meeting, and also established a small interim CIP budget for ADA and related improvements.
points.source: John Lang: So last night City Council did approve resolutions to permanently close Castro Street. John Lang: There's one more step to that, but effectively that is just adopting the ordinance at next week at an October 25th council meeting. John Lang: In addition, council did develop ACIP budget, a small interim budget for some necessary improvements mainly for ADA access and some other elements as part of that.
points.speaker: John Lang

points.text: John Lang said a short-term City Council ad hoc subcommittee made up of Vice Mayor Hicks, Council Member Lieber, and Council Member Madachek would work on pedestrian mall design guidelines, implementation issues, wayfinding, and incorporation of art, with its next meeting anticipated in November.
points.source: John Lang: City Council has developed an ad hoc committee for the pedestrian mall. John Lang: It is a very short term Council committee subcommittee that is looking at the design standards, guidelines, implementation plan and their proposed next agenda is I think all the elements you talk about on wayfinding, incorporation of art. John Lang: That would be Vice Mayor Hicks, Council Member Lieber and Council Member Madachek.
points.speaker: John Lang

points.text: John Lang offered to notify the committee once the next pedestrian mall meeting is scheduled, share what happened at the first meeting along with Gail's report, and place a November VAC discussion on the agenda so the committee can develop recommendations before the ad hoc committee meets.
points.source: John Lang: I can certainly share with you once the next meeting is identified, which is anticipated to be in November. John Lang: I would also be happy to share after this meeting basically what was talked about at the first meeting. John Lang: And then if you want to look at Gail's much more in depth report. John Lang: Yes, I can certainly work to get that agenda.
points.speaker: John Lang

points.text: Committee members described pedestrianization of Castro Street as a major opportunity for public art, including storefront activation, holiday pop-ups, kid-oriented experiences, and closer collaboration with downtown groups to fill vacant storefronts and make art more visible in everyday life.
points.source: Clifford Bryant: I see that as a tremendous opportunity for us to introduce art into the public mindset. Clifford Bryant: Either be on display, be galleries, be studios, be it participation from for kids. Regina Sakols: I think it's an opportunity to coming up now with the holidays. Regina Sakols: Maybe have pop ups.
points.speaker: Clifford Bryant

topics.topic: CIP public art projects status updates

points.text: John Lang reported that the Rengstorff Park Aquatic Center project was about 25 percent complete, remained on track for a late fall 2023 opening, and that the artist was coordinating with the design team.
points.source: John Lang: So on Rangstorf Park Aquatic Center, the project is approximately 25% complete and is on track actually to open late fall 2023. John Lang: The artist has been coordinating with the design team as part of that project.
points.speaker: John Lang

points.text: Fayette Park had just entered construction, and John Lang said there were no major updates beyond the fact that work had started.
points.source: John Lang: The Fayette Park construction just started on the project. John Lang: At this point, there's no significant update other than the project is now under construction.
points.speaker: John Lang

points.text: For the Rengstorff Park maintenance and tennis buildings project, design was wrapping up with a bid expected before year-end, while artist contracts were delayed by insurance issues; John Lang said the mural concept would be brought back once those issues were resolved.
points.source: John Lang: Rangsdorf park maintenance and tennis buildings design is being wrapped up and they do expect that bid to go out before end of this year. John Lang: I continue to work with the artists to get their contracts resolved working through some insurance impediments still. John Lang: Intent is to actually come back to kind of understand what the vision of that mural would look like and function.
points.speaker: John Lang

topics.topic: Public art strategy framework and timeline

points.text: John Lang reviewed the draft public art strategy framework, including a vision centered on diverse public art that enriches Mountain View's culture and vibrancy, supports local artists, and helps position the city as an arts hub.
points.source: John Lang: This was the vision statement. John Lang: The vision for this strategy is the creation of diverse public art enriches Mountain View's innovative and creative culture, enhances the vibrancy of our community, supports local artists, artists and transforms Mountain View into an arts hub.
points.speaker: John Lang

points.text: He also tied the strategy to four earlier public art goals: building community, placemaking, reflecting heritage, and economic development.
points.source: John Lang: The goals were listed there building community, Placemaking, reflecting heritage and economic development.
points.speaker: John Lang

points.text: John Lang grouped the draft strategy around policy and procedure refinement, creating more public art opportunities and placekeeping, and pursuing new partnerships and collaborations with artists and organizations.
points.source: John Lang: One strategy that is going to be deployed is to develop or augment or even refine city policies. John Lang: So develop, augment or refine cities, policies and procedures to better align and encourage art appreciation and instead of placemaking placekeeping. John Lang: And then lastly pursue new opportunities for collaboration with artists and arts organizations.
points.speaker: John Lang

points.text: Examples of proposed initiatives included refining K5 and other policies, developing a fee for private art, using art to improve wayfinding, creating a public art fund, pursuing grants, improving outreach and jury processes, and building a publicly accessible art inventory such as a map, app, book, or archive.
points.source: John Lang: Develop a fee for private art. John Lang: Refine the K5 policy. John Lang: Incorporate art or artistic elements to improve wayfinding within Mountain View. John Lang: Develop a public art fund that supports art appreciation and installations. John Lang: Be more proactive about securing art grants. John Lang: Improve the jury process for greater artistic opportunities and mediums. John Lang: Develop comprehensive, public and publicly accessible art inventory.
points.speaker: John Lang

points.text: Toni Hsu asked that the strategy be framed as a five-year effort so initiatives can be specific and measurable, and John Lang agreed with a five-year horizon followed by a review of progress and possible continuation or evolution of the plan.
points.source: Toni Hsu: Are we, are we thinking this is a five year strategy or 10 year strategy. Toni Hsu: For five years I would want to see some of those initiatives be a little more specific and pointed. John Lang: My inclination is five years. John Lang: At least after five years, there should be a review. John Lang: And these are the goals or milestones we'd like to get to in that five years.
points.speaker: Toni Hsu

points.text: Committee discussion also emphasized treating diversity as a lens across the strategy and broadening partnership language so it can include schools and other organizations, not only formal arts groups.
points.source: Clifford Bryant: We have something that highlights the diversity of the city. Toni Hsu: I think diversity should be a lens that we have on everything we do. John Lang: Maybe softening that language a little bit is exploring those kinds of partnerships instead of being so specific about art organizations.
points.speaker: Toni Hsu

points.text: The committee discussion favored issuing the 2023 Center for the Performing Arts Call for Artists without a theme so as to maximize participation, reduce barriers to entry, and allow selection to focus on quality rather than theme fit.
points.source: Toni Hsu: I prefer not to have a theme. Toni Hsu: I think it's going to make it a lot easier for people to submit their work. Jesse Cupp: If there's no theme, then what that allows us to do is just focus on what is what's the highest quality artwork. John Lang: So it sounds like there's some consensus about no theme this year.
points.speaker: Toni Hsu

points.text: John Lang outlined an operational timeline of issuing the call in late October, making selections in November, and having artist agreements in place by January 1, with exhibits running in roughly 6 to 8 week blocks and with at least six artists plus alternates preferred.
points.source: John Lang: I do look to issue a call here in the month of late October. John Lang: Hopefully have it be done in November with some artist agreements in place beginning of January 1. John Lang: Artists will exhibit anywhere from 6 to 8 weeks in the CPA. John Lang: We need at least 6 but it's better to have two alternates or more.
points.speaker: John Lang

points.text: The committee also discussed keeping the prior broad Bay Area geographic scope and using the public call to mention the city's public art strategy so the announcement doubles as artist outreach.
points.source: John Lang: Currently the city of Mountain View is developing a public art strategy. Jesse Cupp: It's a good way to start doing more outreach. Don Whitebread: It seemed like whatever we settled on last time worked pretty well. Jesse Cupp: I think where it was last time was good.
points.speaker: John Lang

topics.topic: Artist support and community activation proposals

points.text: Members said Mountain View needs more places for local artists to exhibit and sell work, noting that many artists have work to show but few local venues, and that many commissioned artists currently come from outside Mountain View.
points.source: Don Whitebread: It would be nice if we provided more venues for the people that are creating art along with putting up big art. Don Whitebread: They just don't have anywhere to show it. Don Whitebread: The art that we're commissioning is from people at the top of the art pyramid. The real professionals, and that's most of them, aren't from Mountain View.
points.speaker: Don Whitebread

points.text: Regina Sakols added that affordable studio space is also a need for artists, especially for work that cannot be made in small residential spaces, and pointed to studio-space models in nearby cities.
points.source: Regina Sakols: We need studio spaces that are affordable. Regina Sakols: If you want to do sculptures, you need a studio or something. Regina Sakols: Palo Alto has studio spaces. Belmont has studio spaces. Redwood City has studio spaces. San Carlos has.
points.speaker: Regina Sakols

points.text: Toni Hsu and others argued that support for local artists should not be limited to traditional gallery models and could include temporary events, online exposure, art walks, pop-up activity, or other flexible pathways for artists to reach audiences.
points.source: Toni Hsu: Maybe there is a way or mechanism where 1000 artists can show their art. Jesse Cupp: It could be a temporary thing where we have booths. Jesse Cupp: Saturday night art walks would be very cool. Toot Too Thomson: Art walk downtown art walk.
points.speaker: Toni Hsu

points.text: A recurring theme in the discussion was that the city should help create pathways, partnerships, and visibility for artists, while avoiding unnecessary city control over private retail exhibition arrangements that can already happen directly between artists and businesses.
points.source: Toni Hsu: I don't think it's like a program for the city to run. Toni Hsu: The city can help with some marketing, but like a pathway for those artists. John Lang: That can happen any day of the year anytime right now. John Lang: I think it's finding where the city can be a good partner and advocate.
points.speaker: Toni Hsu

points.text: Clifford Bryant said Pyramid Park felt much larger than expected and praised the children's playground, especially the metal canopy and the quality of the play environment.
points.source: Clifford Bryant: One, the park is immense. Clifford Bryant: I was blown away with how the size of it and also the beauty of the children's playground. Clifford Bryant: The metal canopy casting kind of a shaded light on on the ground, just beautifully done.
points.speaker: Clifford Bryant

points.text: He also felt the mobile sculpture was less colorful than expected and visually disappeared against the shiny basketball poles and bright conditions near the courts.
points.source: Clifford Bryant: I was a little bit disappointed with the public art, specifically the mobile. Clifford Bryant: The shininess of the basketball poles and the shininess of the sculpture caused it all kind of to me, the sculpture to get lost.
points.speaker: Clifford Bryant

points.text: Jesse Cupp said the bee-and-butterfly sculpture had limited visibility because of its placement and orientation near the row houses, making part of the piece visible only from a small portion of the park.
points.source: Jesse Cupp: The way it was positioned, the bees are visible for like 5 feet of the park. Jesse Cupp: It's like right at the edge of the park. Jesse Cupp: So it's basically just visible to people who live in those houses back there.
points.speaker: Jesse Cupp

points.text: The discussion raised possible process improvements such as artist check-ins during fabrication, better understanding of park build-out and play equipment earlier in review, site visits, and more regular Public Works participation; a later artist-selection timeline was floated but others noted cost and construction-timing concerns.
points.source: Clifford Bryant: We don't have kind of checkpoints along the way. Clifford Bryant: Should we think about moving the process not at the beginning, but at the end. Don Whitebread: If you got to get all those crews back, it doubles the cost. John Lang: Maybe it's worth doing a field trip. John Lang: It's certainly worth to get our public work teammates back coming to these meetings on a regular basis.
points.speaker: John Lang

points.text: The committee approved a resolution authorizing continued virtual meetings under AB 361 for November so it could retain flexibility if Council returned to in-person meetings after November 1.
points.source: John Lang: Council is passed AB 361, which means it's meeting virtually through November 1st. Jesse Cupp: We should just vote for it to keep our options open and continue to meet virtually even if council decides differently. Jesse Cupp: Motion passes.
points.speaker: Jesse Cupp

points.text: The committee adopted its 2023 meeting schedule for the second Wednesday at 6:00 PM in February, March, April, May, June, September, October, November, and December, with no meetings planned in January, July, or August.
points.source: Jesse Cupp: Second Wednesday of the following months, 6:00 PM. Jesse Cupp: That's February 8th, March 8th, April 12th, May 10th, June 14th, September 13th, October 11th, November 8th December 13th. Jesse Cupp: No meetings are proposed on January, July or August. Jesse Cupp: I'll make the motion. Jesse Cupp: All right.
points.speaker: Jesse Cupp

points.text: John Lang thanked the committee for its work, said he would follow up on the pedestrian mall and ad hoc committee discussion, and expressed appreciation for the committee's feedback on the public art strategy.
points.source: John Lang: I'll certainly follow up with all of you on that. John Lang: I'll follow up on the ad hoc committee as well discussion. John Lang: Really appreciate the feedback this evening.
points.speaker: John Lang

points.text: Jesse Cupp adjourned the meeting at 8:04 PM.
points.source: Jesse Cupp: It's 8:04 PM and the meeting is adjourned.
points.speaker: Jesse Cupp

decisions.text: The committee approved the September 14 meeting minutes by roll-call vote.
decisions.source: Don Whitebread: Can I make that motion. Regina Skalos: I'll second. Jesse Cupp: Can we get a roll call vote please? Jesse Cupp: All right, motion passes.

decisions.text: The committee adopted a resolution authorizing virtual meetings pursuant to AB 361 for November.
decisions.source: Jesse Cupp: I would say we should just vote for it to keep our options open and continue to meet virtually even if council decides differently. Jesse Cupp: I'll make that motion. Jesse Cupp: Motion passes.

decisions.text: The committee reached consensus to issue the 2023 CPA Call for Artists as an open call without a theme.
decisions.source: Toni Hsu: I prefer not to have a theme. Jesse Cupp: If there's no theme, then what that allows us to do is just focus on what is what's the highest quality artwork. John Lang: So it sounds like there's some consensus about no theme this year. John Lang: Just do open call.

decisions.text: The committee adopted the 2023 VAC meeting schedule.
decisions.source: Jesse Cupp: I'll make the motion. Clifford Bryant: I'll second. Jesse Cupp: All right. Jesse Cupp: Thank you.
